sourceMovie.title: Marty Supreme
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Marty Supreme works as a drama movie with a point of view—fans quote moments, but they also argue about what the film is actually saying about power, desire, or survival. The drama and thriller blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: American Psycho
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Fans of Marty Supreme who respond to its drama will find American Psycho (2000) immediately familiar. Both films share a thriller sensibility without ever feeling like carbon copies of each other.

recommendations.title: Lucky Number Slevin
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: There's a reason Marty Supreme fans keep landing on Lucky Number Slevin (2006) — the drama is unmistakably related even when the plots diverge. Both films operate on the same emotional frequency, especially in their third acts.

recommendations.title: Match Point
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Match Point draws from the same drama well as Marty Supreme, channeling thriller in ways that feel unmistakably related. The 2005 film makes a natural companion watch — similar in spirit but distinctly its own.

recommendations.title: Syriana
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Syriana (2005) laid groundwork for the tone and themes that Marty Supreme later expanded. If Marty Supreme resonated for you, this predecessor shows where that emotional DNA began.

recommendations.title: The Wages of Fear
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The drama appeal of Marty Supreme — that tense edge, the thriller — translates cleanly into The Wages of Fear (1953). Different setting, same essential satisfaction.

recommendations.title: The Hummingbird Project
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind The Hummingbird Project (2019) clearly understood what makes drama work — the same quality that keeps Marty Supreme rewatchable. Expect the same tense rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

recommendations.title: The Wolf of Wall Street
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Wolf of Wall Street (2013) is the kind of film Marty Supreme fans discover and immediately send to their group chat. The drama overlap is real, but it's the funny emotional pull that seals the recommendation.

recommendations.title: Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind shares Marty Supreme's gift for drama — the 2004 film delivers science-fiction without overstaying its welcome. Consider it required viewing once you've finished rewatching your first pick.

recommendations.title: Léon: The Professional
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Much of what makes Marty Supreme compelling — the drama, the crime — shows up in Léon: The Professional (1994) in a slightly different form. Both films understand that tense storytelling works best when it trusts the audience.

recommendations.title: Taxi Driver
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The drama energy of Marty Supreme is alive in Taxi Driver (1976), filtered through a different story but the same emotional register. It earns its place on this list with a tense payoff that rivals the original pick.

faq.question: What should I watch after Marty Supreme?
faq.answer: Try American Psycho, Lucky Number Slevin, Match Point—each shares DNA with Marty Supreme while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Marty Supreme?
faq.answer: Marty Supreme (2025) is best described as Drama, Thriller. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Marty Supreme worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 7.4/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this drama lane.
